info.title: Benchmark Validity Assessment Stage 3 (expert_1a429e941728@anonymous.invalid)
info.description:
Thank you for participating in this expert validation study.

You will review automated validity assessments for benchmark datasets in specific deployment contexts. For each assessment, you will:

1. Read a companion PDF with the detailed analysis
2. Answer questions about the assessment's accuracy and completeness

Each section includes a link to the relevant PDF — please open it before answering the questions.

Estimated time: 2.5 - 5 hours.

IMPORTANT: The form has 34 sections. Please aim to take 5-10 minutes on each one.

Please be as transparent as possible with your answers. This will help us accurately frame any contributions or uses of the pipeline in the paper, as well as help us improve the pipeline for future iterations.

IMPORTANT: The total PDF materials are quite long due to the inclusion of detailed evidence tables. You should aim to fully digest the main text of each document, but you do not need to exhaustively read the evidence tables — they are provided as a reference to help you assess your answers to the questions in this form.

Your responses are saved automatically as you progress (requires being signed into your Google account; drafts are tied to your browser and device).

items.title: Assessment 1: MILU — Hindi-Medium Competitive Exam Aspirants — North India (Central Exams)
items.description:
Use case and domain: Deployment scenario: A Hindi-speaking student in India is testing his/her preparation for competitive job-related examinations, with an AI system providing feedback on their responses. The goal is to evaluate the applicability of the benchmark and the quality of the LLM’s feedback.

Domain: Educational assessment
Setting: Mobile application / enterprise software

Note: The deployment hypothesis should be tested using Hindi-language sentences from the benchmark, as I am familiar with the language and can provide evaluation in a subsequent stage.
Target population: A graduate student in North India interacting in the Hindi language. The student has completed their education primarily in Hindi, with limited exposure to English.

Your elicitation responses:

Q1 (IO): "For a graduate student in North India preparing for competitive job exams (e.g., UPSC, SSC, state PSC, banking exams), which subject areas are most critical — General Knowledge, Current Affairs, Indian Polity & Constitution, Hindi language proficiency, regional history of North Indian states (UP, Bihar, Rajasthan, MP), or Mathematics/Reasoning? Are there specific exam types (Central vs. state-level) that your deployment must prioritize?"
  → "Most critical subject areas are General Knowledge, Current Affairs, History, and Mathematics/Reasoning. Additionally, there may be some familiarity required with Indian Polity & Constitution, and proficiency in the Hindi language. To be more specific, let’s focus on the central examinations conducted in India."

Q2 (IC): "Competitive exam questions for North Indian students often draw on culturally specific knowledge — e.g., festivals like Chhath Puja, regional administrative units like tehsils or mandals, state-specific historical figures, or North Indian legal and land-revenue traditions. Does your deployment expect the AI to handle this kind of regionally grounded content, or is the focus on pan-India general knowledge content?"
  → "Central competitive examinations cover both regional aspects as well as pan-India general knowledge content, so AI should be able to handle both sets of knowledge."

Q3 (OO): "When the AI provides feedback on a student's response, what should that feedback look like — a simple correct/incorrect label, an explanation of why an answer is right or wrong, a hint toward the correct answer, or an encouragement message in Hindi? Competitive exam prep often benefits from explanatory feedback, but if the benchmark only scores label accuracy (MCQ), there may be a mismatch with what your deployment needs the model to actually produce."
  → "AI feedback should include a simple correct/incorrect label and an explanation of why an answer is right or wrong in the Hindi language."

Q4 (IC): "Since your target student has limited English exposure and interacts primarily in Hindi, would it be a problem if some benchmark questions — even in the Hindi portion — contain English technical terms, code-mixed phrasing, or assume familiarity with English-medium textbook conventions? Or does your deployment require that all content be fully accessible to a Hindi-medium educated student?"
  → "A small portion of code-mixing with English technical terms is acceptable, but it should not exceed ~10%."

▶ Open the Summary PDF before answering: https://drive.google.com/file/d/1DcZ8SWpT7cIISiyLKH_minFJWnEiR5yX/view?usp=drivesdk

Assessment 2: MILU — North Indian Hindi-Medium Postgraduate Teacher — MCQ Evaluation Deployment
items.description:
Use case and domain: Deployment scenario: A Hindi-speaking teacher in India uses an LLM to evaluate student responses in an examination setting, assign scores with the support of an AI system, and provide feedback.

Domain: Educational assessment
Setting: Mobile application / enterprise software

Note: The deployment hypothesis should be tested using Hindi-language sentences from the benchmark, as I am familiar with the language and can provide evaluation at a subsequent stage.
Target population: A postgraduate- or PhD-qualified teacher with 0–20+ years of teaching experience in Hindi, based in North India and interacting in the Hindi language.

Your elicitation responses:

Q1 (IO): "Your deployment involves evaluating student responses and assigning scores — tasks that require the AI to judge partial credit, borderline answers, and reasoning quality, not just match a correct label. Does your assessment context involve open-ended or long-form student answers (e.g., essay-style, analytical responses), structured short answers, or primarily factual single-item questions? Understanding the mix will clarify whether a benchmark built around multiple-choice exam questions covers the response types your teacher actually encounters."
  → "This proposed evaluation contains only multiple-choice questions where, during evaluation, only one answer is correct. For scoring, positive marks are assigned for correct answers, and negative marks are assigned for incorrect answers."

Q2 (OO): "In Hindi-medium educational assessment in North India, grading often involves nuanced judgment — a student may give a partially correct answer, use a regional Hindi variant, or frame a correct idea unconventionally. Should the AI system support graded scoring (e.g., 0, 1, 2 marks), binary correct/incorrect judgments, or qualitative rubric-based feedback? And would the teacher expect the AI's scoring rationale to be expressed in standard Hindi (Khari Boli / Devanagari-script formal register) or would informal or regional Hindi usage in the AI's output be acceptable?"
  → "Since all the questions are MCQ-type in Hindi, there is only a single correct answer, and grading scores are assigned accordingly"

Q3 (IC): "Hindi instruction in North India spans very different curricular traditions — CBSE, state boards (UP, MP, Rajasthan, Bihar, etc.), and postgraduate university syllabi each carry distinct content norms, canonical texts, and culturally loaded reference points. Does your deployment target a specific board or institution type, or must the AI handle content across multiple curricula? For example, would student responses reference Tulsidas's Ramcharitmanas, Premchand's fiction, or regional historical figures that a benchmark sourced from competitive exams might frame differently than a school or university context would?"
  → "The dplyment should tareger all thr Hindi state/central boards syllabi into the consideration. However, questions may also be referenced from English boards syllabi, provided they are translated into Hindi  and similar to that of Hindi boards."

Q4 (OC): "For the AI's scoring and feedback to be trusted by a highly qualified Hindi teacher, its judgments need to align with what that teacher community would themselves consider correct or acceptable. Are the kinds of answers your students produce — and the mark schemes your teachers use — likely to reflect North Indian academic norms that might differ from how questions were originally keyed in national competitive exam settings? For instance, would a historically or regionally specific answer that diverges from a pan-India 'standard' answer still deserve full credit in your context?"
  → "Most likely no; the evaluation schema across boards and states is almost similar."

▶ Open the Summary PDF before answering: https://drive.google.com/file/d/1fFWK5SWHZSMDHwgG97avKyJ1HlOc6gzY/view?usp=drivesdk

items.title: Are there specific refinements or additions that would make this assessment more useful for practitioners in your domain?
Comparative 1: MILU vs. MMLU
items.description:
The pipeline assessed two benchmarks for the same deployment context: MILU (a benchmark proposed by you) and MMLU (https://arxiv.org/abs/2009.03300) (a benchmark we selected for comparison). Both were evaluated across the same six validity dimensions. The comparative PDF below presents excerpts from each benchmark's individual assessment for this context — please review it before answering the questions.

MILU: 2.2/5 avg | MMLU: 1.2/5 avg
Context: Hindi-Medium Competitive Exam Aspirants — North India (Central Exams)

▶ Open the Comparative PDF: https://drive.google.com/file/d/121tEdWqbpiJMDzfRoi2vxfSYT0tfz7o1/view?usp=drivesdk

Assessment 3: MRBENCH — Metropolitan India — Grades 1–8 Mathematics Tutoring (Student Population)
items.description:
Use case and domain: Deployment scenario: A GPT-4 model is implemented in a one-on-one tutoring session, where its responses are augmented with a human tutor’s input. We need to assesd whather Are the student stisfied with this newly GPT4 responses? 

Domain: Educational tutoring
Setting: Mobile application / enterprise software
Target population: A student from a metropolitan city in India, in grades 1–8, who is fluent in English.

Your elicitation responses:

Q1 (IO): "Your students are in grades 1–8 in metropolitan India. Should the tutoring system handle content tied to the Indian school curriculum (e.g., NCERT-aligned topics, Indian numeral naming conventions like lakhs and crores, word problems referencing rupees or local contexts like train timetables)? Or is the math content entirely curriculum-agnostic in your deployment?"
  → "It should be tied to the Indian school curriculum."

Q2 (IC): "Word problems and tutoring examples in Western benchmarks often use culturally specific contexts (e.g., baseball statistics, US grocery prices, American school scenarios). In your deployment, would students encounter these kinds of culture-distant examples, or would the system generate problems grounded in familiar Indian everyday contexts — like cricket scores, local market transactions, or school fee calculations?"
  → "System should generate problems grounded in familiar Indian everyday contexts."

Q3 (OC): "The benchmark's pedagogical quality labels were annotated by human raters — but your actual measure of success is student satisfaction with GPT-4 responses. Who in your deployment context would be the right judges of a 'good' tutor response: the students themselves (grades 1–8), their parents, or the human tutors co-authoring the responses? Would satisfaction judgments from a metropolitan Indian child likely align with those of the benchmark's annotators?"
  → "The appropriate judges should be human tutors (co-authors) and student engagement. Mostly Yes, satisfaction judgments from a metropolitan Indian child are likely to align with those of the benchmark’s annotators, though some differences may arise due to cultural and educational context."

Q4 (OO): "The benchmark scores tutoring responses on pedagogical dimensions like guidance, tone, and mistake identification — but your deployment goal is student satisfaction, which may not map cleanly onto those dimensions. For your users, would a response that is pedagogically 'correct' but feels overly formal or culturally distant still count as a satisfying response? Or does satisfaction for your students depend on warmth, encouragement, or communication style cues that may be culturally specific to Indian classroom norms?"
  → "No. If a response feels overly formal or culturally distant, it does not count as a satisfying response. It should be pedagogically sound and culturally appropriate."

▶ Open the Summary PDF before answering: https://drive.google.com/file/d/1Ce9Edh6k3lEsxEi3jYGbl_pcN1rqkWnt/view?usp=drivesdk

Assessment 4: MRBENCH — Indian Metropolitan Grade 1–8 Mathematics Teachers (Delhi & Mumbai)
items.description:
Use case and domain: Deployment scenario: A GPT-4 model is implemented in a one-on-one tutoring session, where its responses are augmented with a human tutor’s input. The tutor evaluates the pedagogical quality of the combined response, particularly in terms of how well it provides guidance. The goal is to assess whether the augmented GPT-4 responses are acceptable to the tutor.

Domain: Educational tutoring
Setting: Mobile application / enterprise software
Target population: High-end professional teacher teaching grade 1-8 in major metropolitan cities in India, such as Delhi and Mumbai, who is fluent in English.

Your elicitation responses:

Q1 (IO): "The benchmark focuses on mathematics mistake remediation in a general (Western-default) curriculum context. For your Grade 1–8 teachers in Delhi and Mumbai, do the student mistake scenarios need to reflect the Indian curriculum (e.g., NCERT/CBSE topics, Indian arithmetic conventions like the Indian place-value system with lakhs and crores, or standard algorithms taught differently in Indian textbooks)? Or would curriculum-agnostic math errors be sufficient for evaluating pedagogical quality?"
  → "Curriculum-agnostic math errors be sufficient for evaluating pedagogical quality."

Q2 (IC): "The benchmark's student-tutor conversations were drawn from existing English-language datasets likely reflecting Western classroom dynamics. For your professional teachers in Indian metros, would the depicted student mistakes, student communication styles, or classroom interaction norms feel realistic and recognisable — or do you expect meaningful differences (e.g., more teacher-directed correction styles, deference patterns, or locally common conceptual misconceptions specific to Indian students)?"
  → "Western student–tutor datasets capture many universal conceptual mistakes, but the interaction style often differs from Indian metro classrooms. Indian students tend to be more brief, deferential, and less likely to verbalize reasoning, while teachers may use more direct, exam-oriented correction rather than extended Socratic dialogue. Local contexts also introduce procedure-heavy learning patterns and language-related misunderstandings that are underrepresented in Western data. Overall, the content feels familiar, but the communication dynamics and pedagogy can feel somewhat mismatched"

Q3 (OO): "The benchmark scores pedagogical quality across eight dimensions (e.g., guidance, tone, actionability). For your teachers evaluating whether an augmented GPT-4 response is 'acceptable,' which of these dimensions matter most — and are there dimensions the benchmark may be missing that your teachers would actually judge responses on, such as alignment with the prescribed syllabus, appropriateness of formality in a teacher-student interaction, or compatibility with Indian pedagogical expectations?"
  → "For student mistake remediation, these eight dimensions are expected to be necessary and sufficient. The critical dimension is "providing guidance.""

Q4 (OC): "The benchmark's ground-truth annotations were produced by annotators from a general (likely non-Indian) pool. When your professional Indian teachers evaluate whether a tutoring response provides good guidance, do you expect their judgments to systematically differ — for example, valuing more direct correction over Socratic probing, or prioritising exam-readiness over conceptual exploration — compared to what a Western educator might consider high-quality pedagogy?"
  → "I do expect some differences due to the Indian cultural context, teaching style, pedagogy, etc."

▶ Open the Summary PDF before answering: https://drive.google.com/file/d/1F2Vsrc6mauas5IsrbJgSHAjzrUgvXP8Y/view?usp=drivesdk

Comparative 2: MRBENCH vs. MATHDIAL
items.description:
The pipeline assessed two benchmarks for the same deployment context: MRBENCH (a benchmark proposed by you) and MATHDIAL (https://arxiv.org/abs/2305.14536) (a benchmark we selected for comparison). Both were evaluated across the same six validity dimensions. The comparative PDF below presents excerpts from each benchmark's individual assessment for this context — please review it before answering the questions.

MRBENCH: 3.2/5 avg | MATHDIAL: 3.0/5 avg
Context: Indian Metropolitan Grade 1–8 Mathematics Teachers (Delhi & Mumbai)

▶ Open the Comparative PDF: https://drive.google.com/file/d/1EFpgBIsIk6R_kjmf9jzDMvqpXop_F9AX/view?usp=drivesdk
